sections.heading: Unlawful Access to a Computer.
sections.title: Amendment to financial institution definition in cybercrime act
sections.summary: This section amends Section 27(2) of the Principal Act by replacing the term 'financial institution' with 'any public or private organisation'. The change broadens the scope of entities subject to the cybercrime provisions.

requirements.text: Section 27 (2) of the Principal Act is amended by substituting for the words "a financial arenlinen of institution" in lines | and 2, the words, "any public or private organisation"

sections.source_quote: Section 27 (2) of the Principal Act is amended by substituting for the words "a financial arenlinen of institution" in lines | and 2, the words, "any public or private organisation".

sections.heading: Cyber Terrorssm.
sections.title: Cyber Terrorism Definition
sections.summary: This section defines what constitutes cyber terrorism under Nigerian law. It specifies that cyber terrorism involves acts that threaten national security, public safety, or economic stability through digital means. The definition aids in identifying and prosecuting cyber terrorism cases.
sections.dtia_summary: Provides a legal definition of cyber terrorism, which may affect data transfer assessments involving high-risk data flows.

requirements.text: Cyber terrorism is defined as acts that threaten national security, public safety, or economic stability through digital means

sections.source_quote: 18. Cyber Terrorssm.

sections.heading: Reporting of Cyber threats.
sections.title: Reporting of cyber incidents to National CERT
sections.summary: This section mandates immediate reporting of cyber attacks, intrusions, or disruptions to the National Computer Emergency Response Team (CERT) Co-ordination Center. Failure to report within 7 days results in loss of Internet Services and a mandatory fine of N2,000,000.00 to the National Cyber Security Fund. The CERT may isolate affected systems pending resolution.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires timely reporting of cyber incidents to national authorities to ensure rapid response and mitigation of cross-border data transfer risks.

requirements.text: A Person or Institution operating a Computer System or Network shall immediately inform the National CERT Co-ordination Center of any attack, intrusion or disruption liable to hinder functioning of another Computer System or Network
The National CERT Co-ordination Center may propose isolation of affected Computer Systems or Networks pending resolution of issues

A Person or Institution who fails to report any incident to National CERT within 7 days commits an offence and is liable to denial of Internet Services and payment of N2,000,000.00 fine to National Cyber Security Fund

sections.source_quote: 21.—(1) A Person or Institution who operates a Computer System or a Network, whether Public or Private, shall immediately inform the National Computer Emergency Kesponse Team (CERT) Co-ordination Center of any attack, intrusion and other disruption liable to hinder the functioning of another Computer System or Network, so that the National Computer Emergency Response Team Co-ordination Center can take the necessary measures to tackle the issues.

sections.heading: Cyberstalking.
sections.title: Computer fraud and cybercrime offenses
sections.summary: This section criminalizes the production, supply, adaptation, manipulation, procurement, import, export, distribution, or making available of devices, computer passwords, or programs designed to commit offenses under this Act. Offenders face up to 3 years imprisonment or N7,000,00:00 fine for producing such devices, and up to 2 years imprisonment or N5,000,000.00 fine for possessing unauthorized access codes.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires secure authentication and access controls to prevent unauthorized data transfers across borders.

requirements.text: A Person who unlawfully produces, supplies, adapts, manipulates or procures for use any device, computer program or component designed for committing an offence under this Act commits an offence
A Person who unlawfully produces, supplies, adapts, manipulates or procures for use any computer password, access code or similar data for committing an offence under this Act commits an offence

A Person who unlawfully produces, supplies, adapts, manipulates or procures for use any device designed to overcome security measures in any computer system or network with intent to violate this Act commits an offence
A Person who, with intent to commit an offence under this Act, has in his possession any device or program referred to in subsection (1) commits an offence

A Person who knowingly and without authority discloses any password, access code or means of gaining access to any program or data held in any Computer or Network for unlawful purpose or gain commits an offence

sections.source_quote: 28.—(1) A Person who unlawfully produces, supplies, adapts, manipulates or procures for use, imports, exports, distributes, offers for sale or otherwise makes available— (a) any device, including a computer program or a component designed or adapted for the purpose of committing an offence under this Act, (b) a computer password, access code or similar data by which the whole or any part of a computer system or network is capable of being accessed for the purpose of committing an offence under this Act : or (c) any device, including a computer program designed to overcome security measures in any computer system or network with the intent that the devices be utilized for the purpose of violating any provision of this Act, commits an offence and is liable on conviction to imprisonment for a term of not more than 3 years or a fine of not more than N7,000,000.00 or both.

sections.heading: The objectives of this Act are to— Objectives.
sections.title: Amendment of Principal Act
sections.summary: This section states that the Cybercrimes (Prohibition, Prevention, Etc.) Act, 2015 (referred to as the 'Principal Act') is amended as set out in this Act. It serves as a procedural statement for the amendment process without specifying the changes.

requirements.text: The Cybercrimes (Prohibition, Prevention, etc.) Act, 2015 (in this Act referred Amendment of Act to as "the Principal Act") is amended as set out in this Act

sections.source_quote: The Cybercrimes (Prohibition, Prevention, etc.) Act, 2015 (in this Act referred Amendment of Act to as "the Principal Act") is amended as set out in this Act

sections.heading: The provisions of this Act shall apply throughout the Federal Republic Application.
sections.title: Amendment of Section 17
sections.summary: This section specifies amendments to Section 17 of the Principal Act, including substituting the word 'geniuses' with 'genuineness' and inserting the phrase 'except where they are legally verified in "Certified True Copies"' after the word 'signature'. These changes refine the legal requirements for document verification.

requirements.text: in subsection (2) by substituting for the word "geniuses" the word "genuineness"
in subsection (4) by inserting after the word "signature", the words, "except where they are legally verified in "Certified True Copies"

sections.source_quote: in subsection (2) by substituting for the word "geniuses" the word "genuineness"

sections.heading: The Presidential Order made under Section 3 of this Act may require
sections.title: Amendment to financial institution definition in cybercrime act
sections.summary: This section amends Section 22(1) of the Principal Act by expanding the definition of 'financial institution' to include 'any public or private organisation'. The amendment clarifies the scope of entities covered under the cybercrime provisions.

requirements.text: Section 22(1) of the Principal Act is amended by inserting after the words 'any Financial Institution', the words 'public or private organisation'

sections.source_quote: Section 22 (1) of the Principal Act is amended by inserting after the words "any Financia] Amendment of Institution", the words "public or private organisation".

sections.heading: A Person who, without Lawful Authority, Intentionally or for
sections.title: Amendment to national ID requirement in cybercrime act
sections.summary: This section amends Section 37(1)(a) of the Principal Act by adding 'National Identification Number issued by the National Identity Management Commission and other valid' to the list of acceptable identification documents. The amendment specifies additional valid identification types for cybercrime purposes.

requirements.text: Insert after the word "present" the words "National Identification Number issued by the National Identity Management Commission and other valid"

sections.source_quote: Section 37 (1) (a) of the Principal Act is amended by inserting after the word "present" the words, "National Identification Number issued by the National Identity Management Commission and other valid".

sections.heading: A person who unlawfully destroys or aborts any electronic mail or
sections.title: Amendment to service provider data retention in cybercrime act
sections.summary: This section amends Section 38 of the Principal Act by requiring service providers to retain specific traffic data and subscriber information for two years, in compliance with the Nigeria Data Protection Act and relevant authority prescriptions. The amendment establishes a data retention period for communication services.

requirements.text: A service provider shall keep and protect specific traffic data and subscriber information in accordance with the provision of the Nigeria Data Protection Act and as may be prescribed by the relevant authority for the time being, responsible for the regulation of communication services in Nigeria, for a period of two years

sections.source_quote: A service provider shall keep and protect specific traffic data and subscriber information in accordance with the provision of the Nigeria Data Protection Act and as may be prescribed by the relevant authority for the time being, responsible for the regulation of communication services in Nigeria, for a period of two years.

sections.heading: From the commencement of this Act, any Person being employed
sections.title: Amendment to cyber security coordination in cybercrime act
sections.summary: This section amends Section 41(1) of the Principal Act by adding new requirements for service providers to establish sectoral CERTs and SOCs, route internet traffic through sectoral SOCs, build national cyber forensic capacity, and coordinate international cyber security cooperation. The amendment mandates comprehensive cyber security infrastructure development.

requirements.text: Ensure the establishment of sectoral Computer Emergency Response Teams (CERT) and sectoral Security Operation Centres (SOC) that shall feed into the national CERT
Ensure that all public and private organisations integrate and route their internet and data traffic to the sectoral SOCs thereby protecting the national cyberspace
Establish and maintain a National Computer Forensic Laboratory and coordinate utilisation of the facility by all law enforcement, security, intelligence, law enforcement and military agencies
Build capacity for the effective discharge of the functions of all relevant security, intelligence, law enforcement and military agencies under this Act
Establish appropriate platforms for public private partnership (PPP)
Coordinate Nigeria's involvement in international cyber security cooperation to ensure the integration of Nigeria into the global frameworks on cyber security
Do such other acts or things that are necessary for the effective performance of the functions of the relevant security and enforcement agencies under this Act

sections.source_quote: A service provider shall keep and protect specific traffic data and subscriber information in accordance with the provision of the Nigeria Data Protection Act and as may be prescribed by the relevant authority for the time being, responsible for the regulation of communication services in Nigeria, for a period of two years.

sections.heading: A person who misdirects electronic messages with either the intention
sections.title: Amendment to Section 44 of Cybercrime Act for electronic transaction levy
sections.summary: This section amends Section 44 of the Principal Act by substituting a new levy requirement of 0.5% of all electronic transactions value for businesses listed in the Second Schedule. It introduces new subsections (6)-(8) mandating the Office of the National Security Adviser to administer accounts, maintain records, and ensure compliance monitoring. The Fund's accounts must be audited by the Auditor General for the Federation. Businesses failing to remit the levy face fines of at least 2% of annual turnover and potential closure of their operational licenses.
sections.dtia_summary: This amendment establishes a cross-border data transfer levy mechanism requiring electronic transaction monitoring for businesses operating across Nigeria, impacting data collection and financial reporting obligations for international transactions.
sections.authority: Office of the National Security Adviser

requirements.text: A business specified in the Second Schedule must levy 0.5% of all electronic transactions value
The Office of the National Security Adviser must administer accounts and maintain compliance monitoring records
The Fund's accounts must be audited by the Auditor General for the Federation
Businesses failing to remit the levy face fines of at least 2% of annual turnover
Businesses failing to remit the levy may face closure of operational licenses

sections.source_quote: Section of 44 of the Principal Act is amended — Amendment of section 44 (a) in subsection (1) by substituting for paragraph (a) a new paragraph (a) ... (b) by substituting for subsection (6), new subsections (6) — (8)

sections.heading: A person who knowingly accesses any Computer or Network and
sections.title: Certification of Cybercrime Amendment Act as true copy
sections.summary: This section certifies that the Act is a true copy of the Bill passed by both Houses of the National Assembly, in accordance with the Authentication Act, Cap. A2, Laws of the Federation of Nigeria, 2004. It includes a certification by the Clerk to the National Assembly confirming the authenticity of the document.

requirements.text: This Act may be cited as the Cybercrimes (Prohibition, Prevention, etc.) (Amendment) Act, 2024

sections.source_quote: This Act may be cited as the Cybercrimes (Prohibition, Prevention, etc.) (Amendment) Act, 2024

sections.heading: A Person being authorised by any Financial Institution and charged
sections.title: Financial institution authorized personnel committing fraud via electronic transactions
sections.summary: This section criminalizes individuals or entities authorized by financial institutions to handle electronic financial transactions who commit fraud through false electronic messages. The offense includes posting debits/credits, issuing electronic instructions, and confirming fund transfers. Conviction results in 7 years imprisonment.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires financial institutions to verify authorization of personnel handling electronic transactions to prevent fraudulent data transfers across borders.

requirements.text: A Person being authorised by any Financial Institution and charged with responsibility of using Computer for Financial Transactions commits an offence if they issue false Electronic or Verbal Messages with intent to defraud

sections.source_quote: 20. A Person being authorised by any Financial Institution and charged with the responsibility of using Computer or other Electronic Devices for Financial Transactions such as posting of debit and credit, issuance of Electronic instructions as they relate to sending of Electronic Debit and Credit messages or charged with the duty of confirmation of Electronic Fund Transfer, unlawfully with the intent to defraud, issues false Electronic or Verbal Messages commits an offence and is liable on conviction to imprisonment for a term of 7 years.

sections.heading: A Person being authorised by any Financial Institution and charged (cont.)
A Person being authorised by any Financial Institution and charged (cont.)
sections.title: Card fraud and unauthorized card use offences
sections.summary: This section defines specific offences related to unauthorized card use, including counterfeit access devices, unauthorized access devices, and cards issued to others. Offenders face imprisonment up to 7 years or fines up to N5,000,000.00, with additional obligations to repay losses or forfeit assets derived from fraudulent activities. These provisions apply to individuals and entities involved in card transactions.
sections.dtia_summary: These provisions establish criminal penalties for unauthorized card use, which could impact data transfer assessments by requiring strict controls over cardholder information and transaction data to prevent fraud and ensure accountability.

requirements.text: A Person who uses a counterfeit access device, unauthorized access device, or access device issued to another person resulting in loss or gain commits an offence
Offenders face imprisonment up to 7 years or fines up to N5,000,000.00
Offenders must repay monetary losses or forfeit assets derived from fraudulent activities

sections.source_quote: Cybercrimes (Prohibition, Prevention, Etc.) Act, 2015 (2) A Person who uses— (a) a counterfeit access device; (b) an unauthorised access device; (c) an access device issued to another person. resulting in a loss or gain, commits an offence and is liable on conviction to imprisonment for a term of not more than 7 years or a fine of not more than N5,000,000.00 and forfeiture of the advantage or value derived from his act.

sections.heading: A Person, other than the issuer, who receives and retains possession
sections.title: Receipt of multiple stolen cards
sections.summary: This section prohibits individuals from receiving and retaining possession of two or more cards issued to different cardholders, where they know the cards were stolen. Offenders face imprisonment up to 3 years or fines up to N1,000,000.00 and must repay losses or forfeit assets derived from fraudulent activities. This applies to non-issuer entities handling card transactions.
sections.dtia_summary: This section addresses the handling of stolen cards, which is critical for data transfer impact assessments as it requires strict controls over cardholder information to prevent misuse and ensure accountability in financial transactions.

requirements.text: A Person other than the issuer who receives and retains possession of two or more cards issued to different cardholders, knowing they were stolen, commits an offence
Offenders face imprisonment up to 3 years or fines up to N1,000,000.00
Offenders must repay monetary losses or forfeit assets derived from fraudulent activities

sections.source_quote: A Person, other than the issuer, who receives and retains possession of two or more cards issued in the name or names of different cardholders, which cards he knows were taken or retained under circumstances which constitute a card theft, commits an offence and is liable on summary conviction to imprisonment for a term of 3 years or to a fine of N1,000,000.00 and is further liable to repay, in monetary terms, the value of loss sustained by the cardholder or forfeit the assets or goods acquired with the funds from the account of the cardholder.

sections.heading: A Person, other than an issuer or his authorised agent, who sells a
sections.title: Unauthorized card sales and purchases
sections.summary: This section prohibits unauthorized sales and purchases of cards, where individuals are not issuers or authorized agents. Offenders face fines up to N500,000.00 and must repay losses or forfeit assets derived from fraudulent activities. This applies to entities involved in card transactions outside the issuer's authorization.
sections.dtia_summary: This section addresses unauthorized card transactions, which is critical for data transfer impact assessments as it requires strict controls over cardholder information to prevent fraud and ensure compliance with financial regulations.

requirements.text: A Person other than an issuer or authorized agent who sells a card or buys a card from an unauthorized source commits an offence
Offenders face fines up to N500,000.00
Offenders must repay monetary losses or forfeit assets derived from fraudulent activities

sections.source_quote: A Person, other than an issuer or his authorised agent, who sells a card, or a person who buys a card from a person other than an issuer or his authorised agent, commits an offence and is liable on summary conviction to a fine of N500,000.00 and is further liable to pay, in monetary terms, the value of loss sustained by the card holder or forfeit the assets or goods acquired with the funds from the account of the cardholder.

sections.heading: Where there are reasonable grounds to suspect that the content of
sections.title: Electronic communication interception
sections.summary: This section allows judges to order service providers to intercept, collect, or record electronic communications for criminal investigations. It requires service providers to comply with judicial orders for data collection and provide assistance to law enforcement agencies in investigations.
sections.dtia_summary: This section addresses electronic communication interception, which is critical for data transfer impact assessments as it requires strict compliance with judicial orders to ensure lawful data collection and prevent privacy violations.

requirements.text: Judges may order service providers to intercept, collect, or record electronic communications for criminal investigations
Service providers must comply with judicial orders for data collection

sections.source_quote: Where there are reasonable grounds to suspect that the content of any Electronic Communication is reasonably required for the purposes of a criminal investigation or proceeding, a Judge may, on the basis of information on Oath: (a) order a service provider, through the application of technical means to intercept, collect, record, permit or assist competent authorities with the collection or recording of content data or traffic data associated with specified communications transmitted by means of a computer system; or (b) authorise a law enforcement officer to collect or record such data through application of technical means.

sections.heading: Where there are reasonable grounds to suspect that the content of (cont.)
sections.title: Forfeited assets transferred to Federal Government
sections.summary: The Attorney-General of the Federation must ensure forfeited assets or property from cybercrime offences are effectively transferred and vested in the Federal Government of Nigeria. This obligation applies to the Office of the Attorney-General of the Federation when handling assets seized during investigations under this Act.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires that forfeited cybercrime assets be transferred to the Federal Government, relevant for DTIA when assessing asset forfeiture procedures across borders.
sections.authority: Office of the Attorney-General of the Federation

requirements.text: The Office of the Attorney-General of the Federation shall ensure forfeited assets or property are effectively transferred and vested in the Federal Government of Nigeria

sections.source_quote: The Office of the Attorney-General of the Federation shail ensure that the forfeited assets or property are effectively transferred and vested in the Federal Government of Nigeria.

sections.heading: No.17 Cybercrinies (Prohibition, Prevention, Etc.) Act, 29} :
sections.title: Definitions of key terms in cybercrime legislation
sections.summary: This section provides detailed definitions for terms used throughout the Cybercrimes (Prohibition, Prevention, Etc.) Act, 2015. It specifies what constitutes an Electronic Record, Electronic Transfer of Fund, Expired Card, Financial Institution, Financial Transaction, Function, Identity Theft, Infrastructure Terminal, Interception, Issuer, Law Enforcement Agencies, Minister, Modification, Network, Payment Card, Person, President, Phishing, Purchasing Forged Electronic, Receives or Receiving, Revoked Card, Service Provider, Sexually explicit conduct, Spamming, Traffic, and Traffic Data. These definitions establish the legal framework for understanding cybercrime-related activities and communications in Nigeria.
sections.dtia_summary: These definitions are critical for DTIAs as they establish the precise meaning of terms like 'Electronic Transfer of Fund' and 'Financial Transaction' that directly impact cross-border data transfers and financial processing activities.

requirements.text: Electronic Record means a record generated, communicated, received or stored by Electronic, Magnetic, Optical or other means in an Information system or for transmission from one information system to another

Electronic Transfer of Fund means any Transfer of Funds which is initiated by a person by way of instruction, authorisation or order to a Bank to Debit or Credit an account maintained with that Bank through Electronic means and includes point of sales transfers, Automated Teller Machine transactions, direct deposits or withdrawal of funds, transfer initiated by Telephone, Internet and Card Payment
Expired Card means a card which is no longer valid because the term shown of it has expired

Financial Institution includes any individual, body, association or group of persons, whether corporate or unincorporated which carries on the business of investment and securities, a discount house, finance company and money brokerage whose principal object includes factoring project financing equipment leasing, debt administration, fund management, private ledger services, investment management, local purchase order financing, export finance, project consultancy, financial consultancy, pension fund management, insurance institutions, debt factorization and conversion firms, dealer, clearing and settlement companies, legal practitioners, hotels, casinos, bureau de change, supermarkets and such other businesses as the Central Bank or appropriate regulatory authorities may, from time to time, designate

Financial Transaction means a transaction which in any way involves movement of funds by wire or other electronic means, involves one or more monetary instruments, involves the transfer of title to any real or personal property
Function includes logic, control, arithmetic, deletion, storage, retrieval and communication or telecommunication to, from or within a computer
Identity Theft means the stealing of somebody else personal information to obtain goods and services through electronic based transactions

Infrastructure Terminal includes terminals which include GSM Phones that can be used to access bank or any other sensitive information, Point of sales terminals (POS) and all other Card Acceptor Devices that are in use now or may be introduced in the future

Interception in relation to a function of a computer system or communications network, includes listening to or recording of communication data of a computer or acquiring the substance, meaning or purport of such and any act capable of blocking or preventing any of these functions
Issuer includes a financial institution which or any other entity who is authorised by the Central Bank to issue a payment card
Law Enforcement Agencies includes any agency for the time being responsible for implementation and enforcement of the provisions of this Act
Minister means the Attorney-General of the Federation
Modification means deletion, deterioration, alteration, restriction or suppression of data within computer systems or networks, including data transfer from a Computer System by any means
Network means a collection of hardware components and computers interconnected by communications channels that allow sharing of resources and information

Payment Card means any instrument, token, device, or card, or known by any other similar name, and encoded with a stated money value and issued with or without a fee by an issuer for use of the cardholder in obtaining goods, services, or anything else of value, except money
Person includes an individual, body corporate, organisation or group of persons
President means the President, Commander-in-Chief of the Armed Forces of the Federal Republic of Nigeria

Phishing means the criminal and fraudulent process of attempting to acquire sensitive information such as usernames, passwords and credit card details, by masquerading as a trustworthy entity in an electronic communication through e-mails or instant messaging either in form of an email from what appears from your bank asking a user to change his or her password or reveal his or her identity so that such information can later be used to defraud the user
Purchasing Forged Electronic means a Credit or Debit Transfer Instruments such as Credit Card, Debit Card, Smart Card, ATM or other related electronic payment system devices
Receives or Receiving means acquiring possession, title or control or accepting a card as security for credit
Revoked Card means a card which is no longer valid because permission to use it has been suspended or terminated by the issuer, whether on its own or on the request of the cardholder

Service Provider means any public or private entity that provides to users of its services the ability to communicate by means of a computer system, electronic communication devices, mobile networks; and any other entity that processes or stores computer data on behalf of such communication service or users of such service

Sexually explicit conduct includes at least the following real or simulated acts: sexual intercourse, including genital-genital, oral-genital, anal-genital or oral-anal, between children, or between an adult and a child, of the same or opposite sex; bestiality; masturbation; sadistic or masochistic abuse in a sexual context; lascivious exhibition of the genitals or the pubic area of a child
Spamming means an abuse of electronic messaging systems to indiscriminately send unsolicited bulk messages to individuals and corporate organizations

Traffic means to sell, transfer, distribute, dispense, or otherwise dispose of property or to buy, receive, possess, obtain control of, or use property with the intent to sell, transfer, distribute, dispense, or otherwise dispose of such property

Traffic Data means any computer data relating to a communication by means of a computer system or network, generated by a computer system that formed a part in the chain of communication, indicating the communication's origin, destination, route, time, date, size, duration, or type of underlying service

sections.source_quote: Electronic Record means a record generated, communicated, received or stored by Electronic, Magnetic, Optical or other means in an Information system or for transmission from one information system to another

sections.heading: Offences under this Act shall be extraditable under the Extradition
sections.title: Extradition of cybercrime offences
sections.summary: Offences under this Act are extraditable under the Extradition Act. This applies to the Nigerian government when determining extradition requests for cybercrime cases involving foreign nationals.
sections.dtia_summary: Specifies that cybercrime offences are extraditable, relevant for DTIA when assessing international cooperation in cybercrime cases.

requirements.text: Offences under this Act shall be extraditable under the Extradition Act

sections.source_quote: Offences under this Act shall be extraditable under the Extradition Act.

sections.heading: In this Act—
sections.title: Definitions of cybercrime terms
sections.summary: This section provides definitions for key cybercrime terms, including computer data, computer systems, and cyberstalking. These definitions are used throughout the Act to ensure consistent interpretation of cybercrime offences.
sections.dtia_summary: Provides standardized definitions for cybercrime terms, relevant for DTIA when assessing terminology in cross-border data transfers.
sections.source_quote: Computer Data includes every information required by the computer to be able to operate, run programs, store programs and store information that the computer user needs such as text files or other files that are associated with the program the computer user is running.

sections.heading: 0 Cydercrimes (Prohibition, Prevention, Etc.) Act, 20) 5 (cont.)
sections.title: List of entities for Cybercrime Advisory Council
sections.summary: This section specifies the entities that must be represented in the Cybercrime Advisory Council, including national agencies like the National Agency for the Prohibition of Traffic in Persons, Nigeria Customs Service, Nigeria Immigration Service, and various financial institutions such as banks, insurance companies, and the Nigerian Stock Exchange. It also allows for additional entities to be added by the Minister through the Federal Gazette.
sections.dtia_summary: This section is relevant for DTIAs as it identifies financial institutions and other entities that may be involved in cross-border data transfers and financial transactions, which could impact data flows between these entities.

requirements.text: National Agency for the Prohibition of Traffic in Persons
Nigeria Customs Service
Nigeria Immigration Service
National Space Management Agency
Nigerian Information Technology Development Agency
Nigerian Communications Commission
Galaxy Backbone
National Identity Management Commission
Nigeria Prisons Service

One representative each from the following— Association of Telecommunications Companies of Nigeria; Internet Service Providers Association of Nigeria; Nigeria Bankers Committee; Nigeria Insurance Association; Nigerian Stock Exchange; Non-Governmental Organization with Focus on Cyber Security

sections.source_quote: National Agency for the Prohibition of Traffic in Persons

sections.heading: Citation
sections.title: Certification of Cybercrime Amendment Act as true copy
sections.summary: This section certifies that the Act is a true copy of the Bill passed by both Houses of the National Assembly, in accordance with the Authentication Act, Cap. A2, Laws of the Federation of Nigeria, 2004. It includes a certification by the Clerk to the National Assembly confirming the authenticity of the document.

requirements.text: This Act may be cited as the Cybercrimes (Prohibition, Prevention, etc.) (Amendment) Act, 2024

sections.source_quote: This Act may be cited as the Cybercrimes (Prohibition, Prevention, etc.) (Amendment) Act, 2024

sections.heading: The Cybercrimes (Prohibition, Prevention, etc.) Act, , 2015 (in this Act referred Amendment of Act
sections.title: Amendment of Principal Act
sections.summary: This section states that the Cybercrimes (Prohibition, Prevention, Etc.) Act, 2015 (referred to as the 'Principal Act') is amended as set out in this Act. It serves as a procedural statement for the amendment process without specifying the changes.

requirements.text: The Cybercrimes (Prohibition, Prevention, etc.) Act, 2015 (in this Act referred Amendment of Act to as "the Principal Act") is amended as set out in this Act

sections.source_quote: The Cybercrimes (Prohibition, Prevention, etc.) Act, 2015 (in this Act referred Amendment of Act to as "the Principal Act") is amended as set out in this Act

sections.heading: Section 17 of the Principal Act is amended — Amendment of
sections.title: Amendment of Section 17
sections.summary: This section specifies amendments to Section 17 of the Principal Act, including substituting the word 'geniuses' with 'genuineness' and inserting the phrase 'except where they are legally verified in "Certified True Copies"' after the word 'signature'. These changes refine the legal requirements for document verification.

requirements.text: in subsection (2) by substituting for the word "geniuses" the word "genuineness"
in subsection (4) by inserting after the word "signature", the words, "except where they are legally verified in "Certified True Copies"

sections.source_quote: in subsection (2) by substituting for the word "geniuses" the word "genuineness"

sections.heading: Section 21 (1) of the Principal Act is amended — Amendment of
sections.title: Amendment of Section 21
sections.summary: This section details amendments to Section 21 of the Principal Act, including inserting the phrase 'through their respective sectoral CERTs or sectoral Security Operations Centres (SOC)' after 'Coordination Center' in line 6 and substituting '7 days of its occurance' with '72 hours of its detection'. These changes affect incident response timelines and coordination mechanisms.

requirements.text: in subsection (1) by inserting after the words "Coordination Center" in line 6, the words, "through their respective sectoral CERTs or sectoral Security Operations Centres (SOC)"
in subsection (3) by substituting for the expression "7 days of its occurance", the expression, "72 hours of its detection"

sections.source_quote: in subsection (1) by inserting after the words "Coordination Center" in line 6, the words, "through their respective sectoral CERTs or sectoral Security Operations Centres (SOC)"

sections.heading: Section 22 (1) of the Principal Act is amended by inserting after the words "any Financia] Amendment of
sections.title: Amendment to financial institution definition in cybercrime act
sections.summary: This section amends Section 22(1) of the Principal Act by expanding the definition of 'financial institution' to include 'any public or private organisation'. The amendment clarifies the scope of entities covered under the cybercrime provisions.

requirements.text: Section 22(1) of the Principal Act is amended by inserting after the words 'any Financial Institution', the words 'public or private organisation'

sections.source_quote: Section 22 (1) of the Principal Act is amended by inserting after the words "any Financia] Amendment of Institution", the words "public or private organisation".

sections.heading: Section 27 (2) of the Principal Act is amended by substituting for the words "a financial arenlinen of
sections.title: Amendment to financial institution definition in cybercrime act
sections.summary: This section amends Section 27(2) of the Principal Act by replacing the term 'financial institution' with 'any public or private organisation'. The change broadens the scope of entities subject to the cybercrime provisions.

requirements.text: Section 27 (2) of the Principal Act is amended by substituting for the words "a financial arenlinen of institution" in lines | and 2, the words, "any public or private organisation"

sections.source_quote: Section 27 (2) of the Principal Act is amended by substituting for the words "a financial arenlinen of institution" in lines | and 2, the words, "any public or private organisation".

sections.heading: Section 30 of the Principal Act is amended —
sections.title: Amendment to payment technology definition in cybercrime act
sections.summary: This section amends Section 30 of the Principal Act by adding 'or any other payment technology means' to the list of payment technologies. The amendment expands the scope of payment systems covered under the cybercrime provisions.

requirements.text: In subsection (1) by inserting after the word "terminals", the words "or any other payment technology means"
In subsection (2) by substituting for the words "or point of sales device", the words "or any other payment technology means"

sections.source_quote: Section 30 of the Principal Act is amended — (a) in subsection (1) by inserting after the word "terminals", the words "or any other payment technology means"; and (b) in subsection (2) by substituting for the words "or point of sales device", the words "or any other payment technology means".

sections.heading: Section 37 (1) (a) of the Principal Act is amended by inserting after the word "present".
sections.title: Amendment to national ID requirement in cybercrime act
sections.summary: This section amends Section 37(1)(a) of the Principal Act by adding 'National Identification Number issued by the National Identity Management Commission and other valid' to the list of acceptable identification documents. The amendment specifies additional valid identification types for cybercrime purposes.

requirements.text: Insert after the word "present" the words "National Identification Number issued by the National Identity Management Commission and other valid"

sections.source_quote: Section 37 (1) (a) of the Principal Act is amended by inserting after the word "present" the words, "National Identification Number issued by the National Identity Management Commission and other valid".

sections.heading: Section 38 of the Principal Act is amended by substituting for subsection (1) a new Amendment of
sections.title: Amendment to service provider data retention in cybercrime act
sections.summary: This section amends Section 38 of the Principal Act by requiring service providers to retain specific traffic data and subscriber information for two years, in compliance with the Nigeria Data Protection Act and relevant authority prescriptions. The amendment establishes a data retention period for communication services.

requirements.text: A service provider shall keep and protect specific traffic data and subscriber information in accordance with the provision of the Nigeria Data Protection Act and as may be prescribed by the relevant authority for the time being, responsible for the regulation of communication services in Nigeria, for a period of two years

sections.source_quote: A service provider shall keep and protect specific traffic data and subscriber information in accordance with the provision of the Nigeria Data Protection Act and as may be prescribed by the relevant authority for the time being, responsible for the regulation of communication services in Nigeria, for a period of two years.

sections.heading: Section 41 (1) of the Principal Act is amended by substituting for paragraphs (d) — {h)
sections.title: Amendment to cyber security coordination in cybercrime act
sections.summary: This section amends Section 41(1) of the Principal Act by adding new requirements for service providers to establish sectoral CERTs and SOCs, route internet traffic through sectoral SOCs, build national cyber forensic capacity, and coordinate international cyber security cooperation. The amendment mandates comprehensive cyber security infrastructure development.

requirements.text: Ensure the establishment of sectoral Computer Emergency Response Teams (CERT) and sectoral Security Operation Centres (SOC) that shall feed into the national CERT
Ensure that all public and private organisations integrate and route their internet and data traffic to the sectoral SOCs thereby protecting the national cyberspace
Establish and maintain a National Computer Forensic Laboratory and coordinate utilisation of the facility by all law enforcement, security, intelligence, law enforcement and military agencies
Build capacity for the effective discharge of the functions of all relevant security, intelligence, law enforcement and military agencies under this Act
Establish appropriate platforms for public private partnership (PPP)
Coordinate Nigeria's involvement in international cyber security cooperation to ensure the integration of Nigeria into the global frameworks on cyber security
Do such other acts or things that are necessary for the effective performance of the functions of the relevant security and enforcement agencies under this Act

sections.source_quote: A service provider shall keep and protect specific traffic data and subscriber information in accordance with the provision of the Nigeria Data Protection Act and as may be prescribed by the relevant authority for the time being, responsible for the regulation of communication services in Nigeria, for a period of two years.

sections.heading: Section of 44 of the Principal Act is amended — Amendment of
sections.title: Amendment to Section 44 of Cybercrime Act for electronic transaction levy
sections.summary: This section amends Section 44 of the Principal Act by substituting a new levy requirement of 0.5% of all electronic transactions value for businesses listed in the Second Schedule. It introduces new subsections (6)-(8) mandating the Office of the National Security Adviser to administer accounts, maintain records, and ensure compliance monitoring. The Fund's accounts must be audited by the Auditor General for the Federation. Businesses failing to remit the levy face fines of at least 2% of annual turnover and potential closure of their operational licenses.
sections.dtia_summary: This amendment establishes a cross-border data transfer levy mechanism requiring electronic transaction monitoring for businesses operating across Nigeria, impacting data collection and financial reporting obligations for international transactions.
sections.authority: Office of the National Security Adviser

requirements.text: A business specified in the Second Schedule must levy 0.5% of all electronic transactions value
The Office of the National Security Adviser must administer accounts and maintain compliance monitoring records
The Fund's accounts must be audited by the Auditor General for the Federation
Businesses failing to remit the levy face fines of at least 2% of annual turnover
Businesses failing to remit the levy may face closure of operational licenses

sections.source_quote: Section of 44 of the Principal Act is amended — Amendment of section 44 (a) in subsection (1) by substituting for paragraph (a) a new paragraph (a) ... (b) by substituting for subsection (6), new subsections (6) — (8)
